mn2:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn2:10.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the things that should not be attended to, which he does not attend to?
mn2:10.10: these are the things that should be attended to, which he does attend to.
mn2:10.11: Because of not attending to things that should not be attended to, and because of attending to things that should be attended to, unarisen *āsavas* do not arise in him, and arisen *āsavas* are abandoned.
mn2:10.2: Those things, *bhikkhus*, attending to which an unarisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] grows;
mn2:10.3: an unarisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] grows;
mn2:10.4: an unarisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] grows—
mn2:10.5: these are the things that should not be attended to, which he does not attend to.
mn2:10.6: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the things that should be attended to, which he does attend to?
mn2:10.7: Those things, *bhikkhus*, attending to which an unarisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] is abandoned;
mn2:10.8: an unarisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] is abandoned;
mn2:10.9: an unarisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] is abandoned—
mn2:11.1: He attends appropriately [*yoniso manasi karoti*]: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he attends appropriately [*yoniso manasi karoti*]: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he attends appropriately [*yoniso manasi karoti*]: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he attends appropriately [*yoniso manasi karoti*]: ‘This is the path going to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn2:11.2: For one attending appropriately in this way, three fetters are abandoned—
mn2:11.3: identity view, doubt, and grasping at precepts and observances.
mn2:11.4: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by seeing.
mn2:12.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by restraint?
mn2:12.10: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one dwelling without restraint [*saṁvaraṁ asaṁvutassa*] do not occur in one dwelling restrained [*saṁvaraṁ saṁvutassa*].
mn2:12.11: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by restraint.
mn2:12.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], dwells guarded by restraint of the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*].
mn2:12.3: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one dwelling with the eye *indriya* unguarded [*cakkhundriyasaṁvaraṁ asaṁvutassa*] do not occur in one dwelling with the eye *indriya* guarded [*cakkhundriyasaṁvaraṁ saṁvutassa*].
mn2:12.4: After reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he dwells guarded by restraint of the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*] …
mn2:12.5: he dwells guarded by restraint of the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*] …
mn2:12.6: he dwells guarded by restraint of the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*] …
mn2:12.7: he dwells guarded by restraint of the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*] …
mn2:12.8: he dwells guarded by restraint of the mind *indriya* [*manindriyasaṁvarasaṁvuto*].
mn2:12.9: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one dwelling with the mind *indriya* unguarded [*manindriyasaṁvaraṁ asaṁvutassa*] do not occur in one dwelling with the mind *indriya* guarded [*manindriyasaṁvaraṁ saṁvutassa*].
mn2:13.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by use?
mn2:13.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], makes use of robes:
mn2:13.3: ‘Only for warding off cold, for warding off heat, for warding off the touch of gadflies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things, and only for covering the private parts.’
mn2:14.1: After reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he makes use of almsfood:
mn2:14.2: ‘Not for amusement, not for intoxication, not for adornment, not for beautification, but only for the maintenance and continuance of this body, for ending discomfort, for supporting the holy life, thinking: “Thus I will allay old *vedanā* and will not produce new *vedanā*, and I will keep going, be blameless, and dwell at ease.”’
mn2:15.1: After reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he makes use of lodging:
mn2:15.2: ‘Only for warding off cold, for warding off heat, for warding off the touch of gadflies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things, and only for dispelling the dangers of the weather and for delight in seclusion.’
mn2:16.1: After reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he makes use of medicinal requisites for the sick:
mn2:16.2: ‘Only for warding off arisen painful *vedanās* caused by illness, and for complete freedom from affliction.’
mn2:17.1: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one who does not make use of these do not occur in one who does make use of them.
mn2:17.2: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by use.
mn2:18.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by enduring?
mn2:18.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], is one who endures cold and heat, hunger and thirst, the touch of gadflies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things; he is one with the nature to endure harsh and offensive words, and arisen bodily *vedanās* that are painful, sharp, severe, bitter, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening.
mn2:18.3: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one who does not endure do not occur in one who does endure.
mn2:18.4: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by enduring.
mn2:19.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by avoiding?
mn2:19.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], avoids a wild elephant, avoids a wild horse, avoids a wild bull, avoids a wild dog, a snake, a stump, a thorn patch, a pit, a precipice, a cesspit, a drain.
mn2:19.3: Whatever kind of unsuitable seat, if he sat there, whatever kind of unsuitable resort, if he wandered there, whatever kind of bad friends, if he associated with them, wise fellow practitioners would suspect him regarding bad states—to that unsuitable seat, that unsuitable resort, and those bad friends, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he avoids them.
mn2:19.4: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one who does not avoid these do not occur in one who does avoid them.
mn2:19.5: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by avoiding.
mn2:2.1: “*Bhikkhus*, I will teach you the method for restraining all *āsavas* [*sabbāsavasaṁvarapariyāyaṁ*].
mn2:2.2: Listen to that, attend carefully, I will speak.”
mn2:20.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by dispelling?
mn2:20.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], does not tolerate arisen thought [*vitakka*] of sensuality [*kāmavitakkaṁ*]; he abandons it, dispels it, brings it to an end, makes it disappear; arisen thought [*vitakka*] of ill will [*byāpādavitakkaṁ*] … arisen thought [*vitakka*] of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ*] … whenever evil unskillful *dhammas* arise, he does not tolerate them; he abandons them, dispels them, brings them to an end, makes them disappear.
mn2:20.3: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one who does not dispel these do not occur in one who does dispel them.
mn2:20.4: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by dispelling.
mn2:21.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by development [*bhāvanā*]?
mn2:21.10: These are called, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by development [*bhāvanā*].
mn2:21.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], develops [*bhāveti*] the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*], reliant on seclusion [*viveka*], reliant on fading away, reliant on cessation, culminating in relinquishment;
mn2:21.3: after reflecting with appropriate attention [*paṭisaṅkhā yoniso*], he develops [*bhāveti*] the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhamma* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] …
mn2:21.8: he develops [*bhāveti*] the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*], reliant on seclusion [*viveka*], reliant on fading away, reliant on cessation, culminating in relinquishment.
mn2:21.9: For, *bhikkhus*, the *āsavas*, vexations, and fevers that would arise in one who does not develop [*abhāvayato*] these do not occur in one who does develop [*bhāvayato*] them.
mn2:22.1: When, *bhikkhus*, for a *bhikkhu* the *āsavas* to be abandoned by seeing have been abandoned by seeing, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by restraint have been abandoned by restraint, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by use have been abandoned by use, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by enduring have been abandoned by enduring, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by avoiding have been abandoned by avoiding, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by dispelling have been abandoned by dispelling, the *āsavas* to be abandoned by development [*bhāvanā*] have been abandoned by development [*bhāvanā*]—
mn2:22.2: this is called, *bhikkhus*: ‘A *bhikkhu* dwells guarded by restraint against all *āsavas* [*sabbāsavasaṁvarasaṁvuto*], has cut off *taṇhā*, cast off the fetter, and through fully understanding conceit has made an end of *dukkha*.’”
mn2:22.4: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn2:3.1: “*Bhikkhus*, it is for one who knows and sees that I speak of the destruction of the *āsavas*, not for one who does not know and does not see.
mn2:3.2: And knowing what, seeing what, do I speak of the destruction of the *āsavas*?
mn2:3.3: Appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikāraṁ*] and inappropriate attention [*ayoniso manasikāraṁ*].
mn2:3.4: For one who attends inappropriately [*ayoniso manasikaroto*], *bhikkhus*, unarisen *āsavas* arise, and arisen *āsavas* grow;
mn2:3.5: but for one who attends appropriately [*yoniso manasikaroto*], *bhikkhus*, unarisen *āsavas* do not arise, and arisen *āsavas* are abandoned.
mn2:4.1: There are, *bhikkhus*, *āsavas* to be abandoned by seeing; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by restraint; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by use; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by enduring; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by avoiding; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by dispelling; there are *āsavas* to be abandoned by development [*bhāvanā*].
mn2:5.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the *āsavas* to be abandoned by seeing?
mn2:5.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, who is unskilled in the noble *dhamma* [*ariyadhammassa*], untrained in the noble *dhamma* [*ariyadhamme*], who has not seen persons of integrity, who is unskilled in the *dhamma* of persons of integrity [*sappurisadhammassa*], untrained in the *dhamma* of persons of integrity [*sappurisadhamme*]—
mn2:5.3: does not understand the things that should be attended to, and does not understand the things that should not be attended to.
mn2:5.4: Not understanding the things that should be attended to, and not understanding the things that should not be attended to, he attends to the things that should not be attended to, and does not attend to the things that should be attended to.
mn2:6.1: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the things that should not be attended to, which he does attend to?
mn2:6.10: these are the things that should be attended to, which he does not attend to.
mn2:6.2: Those things, *bhikkhus*, attending to which an unarisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] grows;
mn2:6.3: an unarisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] grows;
mn2:6.4: an unarisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] arises, or an arisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] grows—
mn2:6.5: these are the things that should not be attended to, which he does attend to.
mn2:6.6: And which, *bhikkhus*, are the things that should be attended to, which he does not attend to?
mn2:6.7: Those things, *bhikkhus*, attending to which an unarisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavo*] is abandoned;
mn2:6.8: an unarisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavo*] is abandoned;
mn2:6.9: an unarisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] does not arise, and an arisen *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*] is abandoned—
mn2:7.1: Because of attending to things that should not be attended to, and because of not attending to things that should be attended to, unarisen *āsavas* arise in him, and arisen *āsavas* grow.
mn2:7.2: He attends inappropriately [*ayoniso manasi karoti*] in this way:
mn2:7.3: ‘Was I in the past? Was I not in the past? What was I in the past? How was I in the past? Having been what, what was I in the past?
mn2:7.4: Will I be in the future? Will I not be in the future? What will I be in the future? How will I be in the future? Having been what, what will I be in the future?’
mn2:7.5: Or else he is inwardly perplexed about the present time:
mn2:7.6: ‘Am I? Am I not? What am I? How am I? Where has this being come from? Where will it go?’
mn2:8.1: For one attending inappropriately in this way, one of six views arises.
mn2:8.10: Bound by the fetter of views, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person is not freed from birth, aging, death, sorrows, lamentations, *dukkhas*, distresses, and despairs;
mn2:8.11: I say he is not freed from *dukkha*.
mn2:8.2: Either the view arises in him as true and certain: ‘I have a self’;
mn2:8.3: or the view arises in him as true and certain: ‘I have no self’;
mn2:8.4: or the view arises in him as true and certain: ‘By means of self I perceive self’;
mn2:8.5: or the view arises in him as true and certain: ‘By means of self I perceive not-self [*anattānaṁ*]’;
mn2:8.6: or the view arises in him as true and certain: ‘By means of not-self [*anattanāva*] I perceive self’;
mn2:8.7: or else he has this view:
mn2:8.8: ‘This self of mine, which speaks and feels, which experiences here and there the result of good and bad *kammas*—this self of mine is permanent, stable, eternal, not subject to change, and will remain just so for eternity.’
mn2:8.9: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a view-position, a thicket of views, a wilderness of views, a distortion of views, a writhing of views, a fetter of views.
mn2:9.2: who has seen the noble ones, who is skilled in the noble *dhamma* [*ariyadhammassa*], well trained in the noble *dhamma* [*ariyadhamme*], who has seen persons of integrity, who is skilled in the *dhamma* of persons of integrity [*sappurisadhammassa*], well trained in the *dhamma* of persons of integrity [*sappurisadhamme*]—
mn2:9.3: understands the things that should be attended to and understands the things that should not be attended to.
mn2:9.4: Understanding the things that should be attended to and understanding the things that should not be attended to, he does not attend to the things that should not be attended to, and he does attend to the things that should be attended to.
